draft.introduction: Mark 6:1–6 records Jesus’s rejection at Nazareth, where his neighbors take offense and he “could do no mighty work there.” The passage raises difficult questions about the relationship between faith and divine power. From Codex Sinaiticus.

translation.text: He went away from there and came to his hometown, and his disciples followed him. And on the Sabbath he began to teach in the synagogue, and many who heard him were astonished, saying, “Where did this man get these things? What is the wisdom given to him? Is not this the carpenter, the son of Mary?” And they took offense at him.
translation.original_text: και εξηλθεν εκειθεν και ερχεται εις την πατριδα αυτου

translation.text: And Jesus said to them, “A prophet is not without honor, except in his hometown and among his relatives and in his own household.” And he could do no mighty work there, except that he laid his hands on a few sick people and healed them. And he marvelled because of their unbelief.
translation.original_text: και ελεγεν αυτοις οτι ουκ εστιν προφητης ατιμος

translator_notes.note: Mark’s stark statement that Jesus “could do no mighty work there” (ουκ εδυνατο) troubled later evangelists. Matthew softens it to “did not do” (13:58). The Markan version implies that human receptivity is somehow necessary for divine power to operate.
